name: 1940s
subtitle: Swing & Bebop
description: The big band era gave way to bebop, fundamentally reshaping drumming. Drummers moved timekeeping from the bass drum to the ride cymbal, freeing the rest of the kit for explosive accents. Bebop demanded lightning technique and deep musical conversation. Gene Krupa made the drummer a celebrity; Max Roach made the drummer a composer.

name: 1950s
subtitle: Birth of Rock & Roll
description: Rock and roll erupted from rhythm & blues and country, creating a driving backbeat that changed the world. Drumming became simpler, louder, and more physical. The snare on beats 2 and 4 became the pulse of a new generation. Art Blakey's hard bop pushed jazz drumming into exciting new territory simultaneously.

name: 1960s
subtitle: British Invasion, Soul & Jazz Innovation
description: The 1960s was drumming's most explosive decade of change. The Beatles and British Invasion brought rock drumming to millions. Motown and Stax soul created immaculate pocket-driven grooves. Simultaneously, Elvin Jones reinvented jazz drumming with John Coltrane, Tony Williams blew minds with Miles Davis, and the first stirrings of funk and heavy rock changed everything by decade's end.

name: 1970s
subtitle: Funk, Prog Rock & Heavy Metal
description: Drumming exploded in every direction in the 1970s. Funk created hypnotic, syncopated grooves with ghost notes and 'the one' as the holy grail. Progressive rock pushed complexity to its limits with odd time signatures and extended compositions. Heavy metal and hard rock brought earth-shaking power. Reggae and Afrobeat offered new rhythmic philosophies from outside the Western tradition.

name: 1980s
subtitle: Studio Precision, Speed Metal & Hip-Hop Roots
description: Electronic drum machines entered mainstream music as the Roland TR-808 and TR-909 redefined rhythm. Studio drummers achieved unprecedented levels of precision. Speed metal pushed human physical limits with Lars Ulrich and Slayer's Dave Lombardo. The gated reverb snare became an icon of the era. Hip-hop's foundational breaks were laid down.

name: 1990s
subtitle: Grunge, Hip-Hop & Alternative Explosion
description: Grunge stripped drumming back to primal emotion — loud, raw, and unpolished. Dave Grohl's drumming on Nevermind defined a generation. Hip-hop producers sampled classic Clyde Stubblefield and Funky Drummer breaks into new gold. Metal diversified into death metal, nu-metal, and progressive forms. Alternative rock brought new rhythmic vocabularies from indie to Britpop.

name: 2000s
subtitle: Digital Age & Post-Rock
description: Digital recording and Pro Tools transformed how drums were captured and corrected. Drummers faced new demands for quantized precision alongside authentic feel. Post-rock and progressive metal explored polyrhythm and extended technique. YouTube began democratizing drumming education. Hybrid electronic/acoustic setups became commercially viable.

name: 2010s
subtitle: Streaming, Trap & Genre Fusion
description: Streaming democratized music discovery and drumming followed. Trap hi-hat patterns from hip-hop infiltrated pop and R&B. Hybrid electronic/acoustic kits blurred the line between live and programmed performance. Social media created the viral drum moment — Nate Smith, Larnell Lewis, and others became stars through online clips. Genre boundaries dissolved completely.
